questions.text: Which of the following components are used to define **Positioning Time** (also known as random-access time) in magnetic disks?

choices.label: Transfer rate and Controller overhead
choices.text: Transfer rate and Controller overhead

choices.label: Seek time and Rotational latency
choices.text: Seek time and Rotational latency

choices.label: Cylinder density and Head switch time
choices.text: Cylinder density and Head switch time

choices.label: Buffer depth and Spindle speed
choices.text: Buffer depth and Spindle speed

questions.explanation: Positioning time consists of the seek time (moving the disk arm to the desired cylinder) and rotational latency (waiting for the desired sector to rotate under the head).

questions.text: Calculate the **Average I/O time** for a 4KB block transfer on a 7200 RPM disk with a 5ms average seek time, a 1Gb/sec transfer rate, and a 0.1ms controller overhead. (Assume average latency is half a rotation).

choices.label: Approximately 5.131 ms
choices.text: Approximately 5.131 ms

choices.label: Approximately 9.301 ms
choices.text: Approximately 9.301 ms

choices.label: Approximately 12.450 ms
choices.text: Approximately 12.450 ms

choices.label: Approximately 7.210 ms
choices.text: Approximately 7.210 ms

questions.explanation: Average latency = \( \frac{1}{2} \times (\frac{60}{7200}) \approx 4.17 \) ms. Transfer time = \( 4KB / 1Gb/s \approx 0.031 \) ms. Total = 5 (seek) + 4.17 (latency) + 0.1 (overhead) + 0.031 (transfer) = 9.301 ms.

questions.text: **Select all that apply:** Which of the following are characteristics or advantages of **Solid-State Disks (SSDs)** compared to Hard Disk Drives (HDDs)?

choices.label: Lack of moving parts, resulting in no seek time or rotational latency
choices.text: Lack of moving parts, resulting in no seek time or rotational latency

choices.label: Generally more expensive per megabyte than HDDs
choices.text: Generally more expensive per megabyte than HDDs

choices.label: Infinitely long lifespan compared to mechanical disks
choices.text: Infinitely long lifespan compared to mechanical disks

choices.label: Can be connected directly to the PCI bus for higher throughput
choices.text: Can be connected directly to the PCI bus for higher throughput

choices.label: Lower capacity per dollar than traditional magnetic storage
choices.text: Lower capacity per dollar than traditional magnetic storage

questions.explanation: SSDs have no moving parts, are more expensive per MB, can connect to PCI, and have lower capacity generally. However, they may have a shorter lifespan due to write-cycle limits.

questions.text: What was the access time of the first commercial disk drive, the **IBM Model 350** (RAMDAC system)?

choices.label: Less than 10 milliseconds
choices.text: Less than 10 milliseconds

choices.label: Exactly 100 milliseconds
choices.text: Exactly 100 milliseconds

choices.label: Less than 1 second
choices.text: Less than 1 second

choices.label: Approximately 5 seconds
choices.text: Approximately 5 seconds

questions.explanation: According to the slide content, the IBM RAMDAC model 350 had an access time of less than 1 second.

questions.text: In disk structure mapping, which sector is typically identified as the **first sector** of the first track on the outermost cylinder?

choices.label: Sector -1
choices.text: Sector -1

choices.label: Sector 0
choices.text: Sector 0

choices.label: Sector 1
choices.text: Sector 1

choices.label: The Master Boot Record (MBR)
choices.text: The Master Boot Record (MBR)

questions.explanation: Disk drives are addressed as 1-dimensional arrays of logical blocks where Sector 0 is the first sector of the first track on the outermost cylinder.

questions.text: **Select all that apply:** Which of the following are common I/O bus types or protocols used for **Disk Attachment**?

choices.label: SATA
choices.text: SATA

choices.label: Fibre Channel (FC)
choices.text: Fibre Channel (FC)

choices.label: SCSI
choices.text: SCSI

choices.label: USB
choices.text: USB

choices.label: SMTP
choices.text: SMTP

questions.explanation: SATA, Fibre Channel, SCSI, and USB are all mentioned as valid disk attachment/bus technologies. SMTP is a mail protocol.

questions.text: What is the primary function of **LUN Masking** in a Storage Area Network (SAN)?

choices.label: To encrypt data packets over Fibre Channel
choices.text: To encrypt data packets over Fibre Channel

choices.label: To allocate and restrict specific storage units to specific servers
choices.text: To allocate and restrict specific storage units to specific servers

choices.label: To hide bad sectors from the operating system
choices.text: To hide bad sectors from the operating system

choices.label: To compress data before it reaches the disk controller
choices.text: To compress data before it reaches the disk controller

questions.explanation: LUN Masking makes storage available from specific arrays to specific servers in a SAN environment.

questions.text: How does **Network-Attached Storage (NAS)** differ from a traditional **Storage Area Network (SAN)**?

choices.label: NAS provides block-level access while SAN provides file-level access
choices.text: NAS provides block-level access while SAN provides file-level access

choices.label: NAS uses NFS/CIFS protocols over IP; SAN typically uses Fibre Channel or iSCSI for block-level access
choices.text: NAS uses NFS/CIFS protocols over IP; SAN typically uses Fibre Channel or iSCSI for block-level access

choices.label: NAS is always faster than SAN due to TCP/IP optimization
choices.text: NAS is always faster than SAN due to TCP/IP optimization

choices.label: SAN is only for backups, whereas NAS is for primary storage
choices.text: SAN is only for backups, whereas NAS is for primary storage

questions.explanation: NAS provides file-system level access (NFS/CIFS) over a network, whereas SAN provides block-level access via Fibre Channel or specialized protocols like iSCSI.

questions.text: In the context of disk scheduling, **Disk Bandwidth** is defined as:

choices.label: The maximum theoretical speed of the SATA/SCSI bus
choices.text: The maximum theoretical speed of the SATA/SCSI bus

choices.label: The total number of bytes transferred divided by the total time between the first request and completion of the last transfer
choices.text: The total number of bytes transferred divided by the total time between the first request and completion of the last transfer

choices.label: The rotational speed of the platters measured in RPM
choices.text: The rotational speed of the platters measured in RPM

choices.label: The time taken to switch between the top and bottom heads of a platter
choices.text: The time taken to switch between the top and bottom heads of a platter

questions.explanation: Bandwidth is a measure of effective data throughput: total bytes transferred divided by total time from first request to final completion.

questions.text: Given a disk queue with requests at **98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67** and the head at **53**, which request will the **SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First)** algorithm service first?
questions.explanation: SSTF chooses the request closest to the current head position. |53-65|=12, which is smaller than |53-37|=16 or |53-67|=14. Therefore, 65 is serviced first.

questions.text: The **SCAN** algorithm is frequently referred to as the:

choices.label: Shortest-Job-First algorithm
choices.text: Shortest-Job-First algorithm

choices.label: Elevator algorithm
choices.text: Elevator algorithm

choices.label: Circular list algorithm
choices.text: Circular list algorithm

choices.label: First-Come-First-Served algorithm
choices.text: First-Come-First-Served algorithm

questions.explanation: SCAN is called the elevator algorithm because it moves from one end of the disk to the other, servicing requests, similar to an elevator.

questions.text: What is the key difference between the **SCAN** and **C-SCAN** disk scheduling algorithms?

choices.label: C-SCAN services requests in both directions of head movement
choices.text: C-SCAN services requests in both directions of head movement

choices.label: C-SCAN immediately returns to the beginning of the disk without servicing requests on the return trip
choices.text: C-SCAN immediately returns to the beginning of the disk without servicing requests on the return trip

choices.label: SCAN provides a more uniform wait time than C-SCAN
choices.text: SCAN provides a more uniform wait time than C-SCAN

choices.label: C-SCAN only services the outermost cylinder
choices.text: C-SCAN only services the outermost cylinder

questions.explanation: C-SCAN (Circular SCAN) is designed to provide more uniform wait times by only servicing requests in one direction and jumping back to the start when the end is reached.

questions.text: In the **C-LOOK** algorithm, how far does the disk arm move?

choices.label: To the very end of the disk (cylinder 0 and 199)
choices.text: To the very end of the disk (cylinder 0 and 199)

choices.label: Only as far as the last request in each direction
choices.text: Only as far as the last request in each direction

choices.label: It stays at the center cylinder and waits for requests
choices.text: It stays at the center cylinder and waits for requests

choices.label: It moves in a random pattern to avoid starvation
choices.text: It moves in a random pattern to avoid starvation

questions.explanation: LOOK and C-LOOK are versions of SCAN/C-SCAN that 'look' for the last request in the current direction and reverse/return immediately without going to the physical end of the disk.

questions.text: **Select all that apply:** Which of the following features are commonly found in advanced **Storage Arrays**?

choices.label: Snapshots
choices.text: Snapshots

choices.label: Deduplication
choices.text: Deduplication

choices.label: Thin provisioning
choices.text: Thin provisioning

choices.label: Replication
choices.text: Replication

choices.label: CPU Overclocking
choices.text: CPU Overclocking

questions.explanation: Snapshots, deduplication, thin provisioning, and replication are standard advanced storage array features mentioned in the slides.

questions.text: What is the purpose of **Low-level formatting** (physical formatting)?

choices.label: To install the operating system kernel
choices.text: To install the operating system kernel

choices.label: To divide the disk into sectors with headers, ECC, and data areas
choices.text: To divide the disk into sectors with headers, ECC, and data areas

choices.label: To create the NTFS or ext4 file system structures
choices.text: To create the NTFS or ext4 file system structures

choices.label: To partition the disk into logical drives
choices.text: To partition the disk into logical drives

questions.explanation: Low-level formatting divides the physical media into sectors that the disk controller can recognize, including overhead for ECC and headers.

questions.text: Where is the **Bootstrap loader** program typically stored?

choices.label: In the swap space
choices.text: In the swap space

choices.label: In the bad sector map
choices.text: In the bad sector map

choices.label: In the boot blocks of the boot partition
choices.text: In the boot blocks of the boot partition

choices.label: In the L1 Cache of the CPU
choices.text: In the L1 Cache of the CPU

questions.explanation: The bootstrap loader is stored in the boot blocks of the boot partition on the disk.

questions.text: **Select all that apply:** Which of the following are true regarding **Swap-Space Management**?

choices.label: Swap space can be a dedicated raw partition or a file within the file system
choices.text: Swap space can be a dedicated raw partition or a file within the file system

choices.label: Solaris 2 only allocates swap space when a dirty page is forced out of physical memory
choices.text: Solaris 2 only allocates swap space when a dirty page is forced out of physical memory

choices.label: Swap space is used to extend the capacity of main memory (Virtual Memory)
choices.text: Swap space is used to extend the capacity of main memory (Virtual Memory)

choices.label: Swap space is generally faster than physical RAM
choices.text: Swap space is generally faster than physical RAM

choices.label: Linux uses specific data structures like swap maps to track usage
choices.text: Linux uses specific data structures like swap maps to track usage

questions.explanation: Swap space is slower than RAM (as it is on disk). The other points regarding allocation strategies and implementation are correct.

questions.text: If a disk has a **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** of 100,000 hours and a **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** of 10 hours, what is the Mean Time to Data Loss for a mirrored (RAID 1) system?

choices.label: 100,000 hours
choices.text: 100,000 hours

questions.explanation: Using the formula from the slide: \( 100,000^2 / (2 \times 10) = 500 	imes 10^6 \) hours.

questions.text: Which RAID level provides **Block Interleaved Parity** and is commonly used to balance performance and reliability with less redundancy than mirroring?

choices.label: RAID 0
choices.text: RAID 0

choices.label: RAID 1
choices.text: RAID 1

choices.label: RAID 5
choices.text: RAID 5

choices.label: RAID 1+0
choices.text: RAID 1+0

questions.explanation: RAID 5 uses block-interleaved parity across multiple disks to provide fault tolerance without the 100% overhead of RAID 1.

questions.text: What is the difference between **RAID 0+1** and **RAID 1+0**?

choices.label: RAID 0+1 is a stripe of mirrors, while RAID 1+0 is a mirror of stripes
choices.text: RAID 0+1 is a stripe of mirrors, while RAID 1+0 is a mirror of stripes

choices.label: RAID 1+0 is a stripe of mirrors, while RAID 0+1 is a mirror of stripes
choices.text: RAID 1+0 is a stripe of mirrors, while RAID 0+1 is a mirror of stripes

choices.label: RAID 0+1 does not provide any redundancy
choices.text: RAID 0+1 does not provide any redundancy

choices.label: They are identical in fault tolerance and performance
choices.text: They are identical in fault tolerance and performance

questions.explanation: RAID 1+0 (Striped Mirrors) stripes data across sets of mirrored disks. RAID 0+1 (Mirrored Stripes) mirrors two large striped sets.

questions.text: **Select all that apply:** Which of the following features does the **Solaris ZFS** file system provide for data integrity?

choices.label: Checksums of all data and metadata
choices.text: Checksums of all data and metadata

choices.label: Pooled storage (removing traditional partitions)
choices.text: Pooled storage (removing traditional partitions)

choices.label: Detection and correction of data corruption
choices.text: Detection and correction of data corruption

choices.label: Manual sector sparing via a physical jumper
choices.text: Manual sector sparing via a physical jumper

choices.label: Direct PCI-to-Disk hardware bypass
choices.text: Direct PCI-to-Disk hardware bypass

questions.explanation: ZFS uses checksums to detect/correct corruption and uses a pooled storage model.

questions.text: In the implementation of **Stable Storage**, what are the three possible outcomes of a disk write?

choices.label: Success, Cached, Deleted
choices.text: Success, Cached, Deleted

choices.label: Successful completion, Partial failure, Total failure
choices.text: Successful completion, Partial failure, Total failure

choices.label: Seek error, Latency error, Write error
choices.text: Seek error, Latency error, Write error

choices.label: Synchronous, Asynchronous, Mirroring
choices.text: Synchronous, Asynchronous, Mirroring

questions.explanation: According to the slides, a disk write results in successful completion, partial failure (mid-transfer failure), or total failure (failure before write starts).

questions.text: To achieve **Stable Storage**, the system must maintain how many physical blocks for every logical block?

choices.label: RAID 5 parity only
choices.text: RAID 5 parity only

questions.explanation: Stable storage is implemented by maintaining 2 physical blocks for each logical block and updating them in a specific sequence (write to 1st, then write to 2nd).

questions.text: What is a **Hot Spare** disk used for in a RAID array?

choices.label: To increase the transfer rate during peak hours
choices.text: To increase the transfer rate during peak hours

choices.label: To serve as a high-speed cache for the controller
choices.text: To serve as a high-speed cache for the controller

choices.label: To automatically replace a failed disk and rebuild the RAID set
choices.text: To automatically replace a failed disk and rebuild the RAID set

choices.label: To store the operating system kernel for faster booting
choices.text: To store the operating system kernel for faster booting

questions.explanation: Hot spares are unallocated disks that the system can use to immediately begin rebuilding data if a production disk fails.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a **Head Crash**?

choices.label: The disk controller running out of memory
choices.text: The disk controller running out of memory

choices.label: The disk head making physical contact with the disk surface
choices.text: The disk head making physical contact with the disk surface

choices.label: A failure in the SATA bus cable
choices.text: A failure in the SATA bus cable

choices.label: The spindle motor rotating too fast
choices.text: The spindle motor rotating too fast

questions.explanation: A head crash occurs when the read-write head touches the platter surface, usually damaging both the head and the media.
